title: Node.js Buffers & Binary Data
description: How `Buffer`, `Uint8Array`, `ArrayBuffer`, and `DataView` fit together; UTF-8 / hex / base64 encodings; byte order; which operations share memory versus copy; and why turning binary into a string destroys it. Every answer is verified by running the code in Node.

questions.title: `Buffer` is a subclass of which built-in JavaScript typed array?
questions.description: Name the standard typed array that Node's `Buffer` extends, so that every Buffer is also an instance of it.
questions.explanation:
Node's `Buffer` is not a separate language type bolted on — it is a **subclass of `Uint8Array`**, the standard array-of-bytes view. You can confirm it directly:

```js
Buffer.from([1, 2, 3]) instanceof Uint8Array;                      // true
Object.getPrototypeOf(Buffer.prototype) === Uint8Array.prototype;  // true
```

The relationship is **one-way**: every Buffer is a Uint8Array, but a plain Uint8Array is not a Buffer (`new Uint8Array(1) instanceof Buffer` is `false`). Because a Buffer *is* a Uint8Array, you can hand one to any standard API that expects a Uint8Array — `TextDecoder`, Web Crypto, `fetch` bodies. Buffer simply adds Node conveniences on top: string encodings, fixed-width integer reads like `readInt32BE`, `toString('hex')`, and an allocation pool.

questions.title: What is an `ArrayBuffer`, and how do you read the bytes inside it?
questions.description: You write `const ab = new ArrayBuffer(8)`. How do you access the individual bytes?

options.text: It is a fixed-length block of raw memory with no element accessors; you read it through a *view* such as a `TypedArray` or `DataView`.
It is itself an array of bytes; you index it directly with `ab[0]`.
It is a growable list of numbers; you call `ab.push(byte)` to append data.
It is a UTF-8 string buffer; you call `ab.toString()` to read its contents.

questions.explanation:
An `ArrayBuffer` is just a fixed-size chunk of raw memory. It deliberately exposes **no way to read or write its contents directly** — `ab[0]` is `undefined` and there is no `.push`. To touch the bytes you wrap it in a *view*:

```js
const ab = new ArrayBuffer(8);
ab[0];                         // undefined — no element access
const bytes = new Uint8Array(ab); // a view over the same 8 bytes
bytes[0] = 255;                // now you can read and write
new DataView(ab).getUint8(0);  // 255 — a different view of the same memory
```

This split is the heart of the binary model: one `ArrayBuffer` owns the memory, and many views (`Uint8Array`, `Float64Array`, `DataView`) can interpret that same memory in different ways. A Node `Buffer` is one such view — a `Uint8Array` — with extra methods.

questions.title: What is `new Uint32Array(4).byteLength`?
questions.description: Four 32-bit unsigned integers. Give the total number of *bytes* the typed array occupies.
questions.explanation:
`length` counts *elements*; `byteLength` counts *bytes*. A `Uint32Array` stores 32-bit (4-byte) integers, so 4 elements occupy 4 × 4 = **16 bytes**.

```js
const a = new Uint32Array(4);
a.length;                      // 4  (elements)
a.byteLength;                  // 16 (bytes)
Uint32Array.BYTES_PER_ELEMENT; // 4
```

This is exactly why the view type matters: one 16-byte `ArrayBuffer` is 16 elements as a `Uint8Array`, 8 as a `Uint16Array`, 4 as a `Uint32Array`, or 2 as a `Float64Array`. For a Node `Buffer` (a `Uint8Array`), `length` and `byteLength` are always equal because every element is a single byte.

questions.title: Which statements about `Buffer` versus `Uint8Array` are correct?
questions.description: Select every true statement about how Node's `Buffer` relates to the standard `Uint8Array`.

options.text: `Buffer` extends `Uint8Array`, so `buf instanceof Uint8Array` is `true` and a Buffer works anywhere a Uint8Array is accepted.
`Buffer` adds methods a plain `Uint8Array` lacks, such as `readInt32BE`, `write`, and `toString('hex')`.
`Buffer` is a Node.js global, whereas `Uint8Array` is a standard JavaScript built-in that also exists in browsers.
A plain `Uint8Array` is an instance of `Buffer`, so `new Uint8Array(8) instanceof Buffer` is `true`.

questions.explanation:
The first three are true; the subclass relationship runs in **one direction only**.

```js
Buffer.from([1]) instanceof Uint8Array;  // true  (Buffer is a Uint8Array)
new Uint8Array(1) instanceof Buffer;     // false (but not the reverse)
typeof Uint8Array.prototype.readInt32BE; // 'undefined' — a Buffer-only method
Buffer.from([1, 2, 3]).toString('hex');  // '010203'
new Uint8Array([1, 2, 3]).toString();    // '1,2,3' — inherited Array-style join
```

Mental model: `Buffer` = `Uint8Array` + Node conveniences (string encodings, fixed-width integer reads and writes, an internal allocation pool). Prefer a plain `Uint8Array` in code meant to run in both Node and the browser; reach for `Buffer` when you want its encoding and numeric helpers.

questions.title: How many bytes is the string `café` in UTF-8?
questions.description: Give the result of `Buffer.byteLength('café', 'utf8')` — the number of bytes, not the number of characters.
questions.explanation:
`c`, `a`, and `f` are one byte each in UTF-8, but `é` (U+00E9) needs **two** bytes (`0xC3 0xA9`), so the total is **5 bytes** — even though the string is only 4 characters long.

```js
'café'.length;                     // 4  (UTF-16 code units / characters)
Buffer.byteLength('café', 'utf8'); // 5  (bytes on the wire)
Buffer.from('café').toString('hex'); // '636166c3a9'
```

This gap between *character count* and *byte count* is the root of countless bugs: a `Content-Length` header, a fixed-size buffer, or a substring offset computed from `.length` will all be wrong for non-ASCII text. When you need bytes, measure bytes with `Buffer.byteLength`, not `String.length`.

questions.title: What does `Buffer.from('SGVsbG8=', 'base64').toString('utf8')` return?
questions.description: Decode the Base64 text `SGVsbG8=` back to a UTF-8 string and type the result.
questions.explanation:
`Buffer` is Node's converter between encodings. The Base64 text `SGVsbG8=` decodes to the five bytes `48 65 6c 6c 6f`, which are the ASCII codes for `Hello`.

```js
Buffer.from('SGVsbG8=', 'base64').toString('utf8'); // 'Hello'
Buffer.from('Hello').toString('base64');            // 'SGVsbG8=' (round-trip)
Buffer.from('Hello').toString('hex');               // '48656c6c6f'
```

The pattern is always *bytes in one encoding → Buffer → bytes out in another*: `Buffer.from(input, fromEncoding)` then `.toString(toEncoding)`. Supported encodings include `utf8`, `hex`, `base64`, `base64url`, `latin1`, and `ascii`. Note the Buffer is the real value here — the strings are just two different textual *representations* of the same underlying bytes.

questions.title: Reading a binary file, you write `let data = ''` then `stream.on('data', c => data += c)`. Why is the result corrupted?
questions.description: You accumulate raw binary chunks into a string with `+=` instead of collecting Buffers. What goes wrong?

options.text: `data += chunk` decodes each Buffer to UTF-8; bytes that are not valid UTF-8 become the replacement character `�`, and that loss is irreversible.
String concatenation is too slow, so chunks arrive faster than they can be appended and some are dropped.
`+=` keeps only the last chunk because strings are immutable, so earlier data is overwritten.
The bytes are fine in memory; the data only looks wrong when printed because the terminal cannot display binary.

questions.title: Decoding a UTF-8 stream as `chunk.toString()` per chunk sometimes yields `�`. Why?
questions.description: Each chunk is decoded on its own and the resulting strings are concatenated. A multi-byte character can land exactly on a chunk boundary.

options.text: A multi-byte character can be split across two chunks; each half is invalid UTF-8 alone and decodes to `�`. Concatenate the *bytes* first, or use `StringDecoder`.
`toString()` randomly fails on long inputs, so some characters are lost.
UTF-8 is not supported by `Buffer.toString()`; you must pass `'utf16'` to avoid `�`.
The stream delivers chunks out of order, scrambling the characters.

questions.explanation:
A stream splits data by *byte count*, with no regard for character boundaries. The euro sign `€` is the three bytes `e2 82 ac`; if one chunk ends after `e2 82` and the next begins with `ac`, decoding each chunk on its own produces two invalid fragments:

```js
const euro = Buffer.from('€');      // <Buffer e2 82 ac>
const c1 = euro.subarray(0, 2);     // e2 82  (incomplete sequence)
const c2 = euro.subarray(2);        // ac     (orphan continuation byte)
c1.toString() + c2.toString();      // '��'  — corrupted, each half is invalid
Buffer.concat([c1, c2]).toString(); // '€'   — join the bytes, then decode once
```

Two correct fixes: (1) buffer the raw chunks and decode once at the end with `Buffer.concat`, or (2) use Node's `string_decoder`, which holds back an incomplete trailing sequence until the rest arrives:

```js
const { StringDecoder } = require('node:string_decoder');
const d = new StringDecoder('utf8');
d.write(c1); // ''   — withholds the partial character
d.write(c2); // '€'  — completes it on the next chunk
```

questions.title: What is `[...new Uint8Array([256, -1, 300])]`?
questions.description: A `Uint8Array` holds bytes in the range 0–255. What happens to out-of-range initializer values?

options.text: `[0, 255, 44]` — each value is taken modulo 256 (only the low 8 bits are kept).
`[255, 0, 255]` — values are clamped into the 0–255 range.
`[256, -1, 300]` — the numbers are stored unchanged.
It throws a `RangeError` because the values do not fit in a byte.

questions.explanation:
Writing to a `Uint8Array` keeps only the low 8 bits — it **wraps modulo 256** rather than clamping or throwing:

```js
[...new Uint8Array([256, -1, 300])]; // [0, 255, 44]
// 256 % 256 = 0,  -1 wraps to 255,  300 % 256 = 44
Buffer.from([257])[0];               // 1  — Buffer is a Uint8Array, same rule
```

If you want *saturation* instead (negatives become 0, values above 255 become 255), that is a different type — `Uint8ClampedArray`, used by the Canvas API for pixel data:

```js
[...new Uint8ClampedArray([256, -1, 300])]; // [255, 0, 255]
```

Knowing which rule applies prevents a class of silent bugs where a computed byte quietly wraps around instead of raising an error.

questions.title: The bytes `00 00 00 01` read as a 32-bit integer give `1` one way and `16777216` the other. What causes the difference?
questions.description: Same four bytes, two different numbers. What determines which value you get?

options.text: Byte order (endianness): big-endian reads `00 00 00 01` as `1`; little-endian reads it as `0x01000000` = `16777216`.
Signedness: one reading interprets the bytes as signed and the other as unsigned.
Number base: one result is hexadecimal and the other is decimal.
A misread: `16777216` means the four bytes were misaligned in memory.

questions.explanation:
A multi-byte integer can be laid out *most-significant byte first* (big-endian) or *least-significant byte first* (little-endian). The same bytes therefore encode different numbers:

```js
const buf = Buffer.from([0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x01]);
buf.readUInt32BE(0); // 1         — 0x00000001
buf.readUInt32LE(0); // 16777216  — 0x01000000
```

This is why `DataView` exists and why a plain `Uint32Array` is risky for *external* data: `DataView` forces you to state the byte order, but a `Uint32Array` silently uses the host platform's order (little-endian on x86 and ARM):

```js
const ab = Uint8Array.of(0, 0, 0, 1).buffer;
new DataView(ab).getUint32(0);       // 1        (big-endian is the default)
new DataView(ab).getUint32(0, true); // 16777216 (littleEndian = true)
new Uint32Array(ab)[0];              // 16777216 (whatever the CPU uses)
```

Network protocols and many binary file formats are big-endian (`network byte order`), so decode them with an explicit big-endian reader like `readUInt32BE` rather than trusting the platform default.

questions.title: Which of these operations return something that *shares* memory with the source rather than copying it?
questions.description: Sharing memory means a write through one reference is visible through the other. Select every operation that shares.

options.text: `buf.subarray(0, 2)` — a view onto the same bytes, so writing to it changes `buf`.
`Buffer.from(arrayBuffer)` — wraps the existing `ArrayBuffer` in place, with no copy.
`Buffer.concat([a, b])` — its result stays linked to `a` and `b`.
`new Uint8Array(buf)` — its result stays linked to `buf`.

questions.title: What is the difference between `Buffer.alloc(1024)` and `Buffer.allocUnsafe(1024)`?
questions.description: Both create a 1 KB Buffer. One is faster; one is safe to read immediately. Which is which, and why?

options.text: `alloc` zero-fills the memory; `allocUnsafe` skips that step for speed, so it may expose leftover bytes from previously used memory.
`allocUnsafe` can be freed manually while `alloc` is garbage-collected — the names are about memory management.
`allocUnsafe` may throw when memory is low; `alloc` never throws.
`alloc` creates a fixed-size Buffer; `allocUnsafe` creates a growable one.

questions.explanation:
Both return a 1024-byte Buffer, but `Buffer.alloc` **zero-fills** it first, while `Buffer.allocUnsafe` returns a region of (often pooled) memory **without clearing it** — so it can still hold whatever bytes were there before, including data from elsewhere in your process:

```js
Buffer.alloc(8).toString('hex'); // '0000000000000000' — always zeroed
Buffer.allocUnsafe(8);           // e.g. <Buffer a0 3f 00 ...> — leftover bytes
```

That is the `unsafe` part: it is faster (it skips the fill and may reuse Node's internal memory pool), but if you transmit or persist it before overwriting every byte, you can **leak sensitive data**. Rule of thumb: default to `Buffer.alloc`; only use `allocUnsafe` when you will immediately and completely overwrite the buffer — for example, reading into it from a file or socket.
